narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 40% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on your profile: ranking in the lowest decile for Year 1 grades at school (compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack 6th decile), a 2nd decile for Year 3 grades (versus the average 5th or 6th), an LSAT score of 36 (a bit below the usual 37), a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.5 (stronger than the average 3.2), upper-middle family income, identifying as female and White, and planning to attend part-time. The model weighed these factors carefully, with your Year 3 school ranking having the single biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure, followed closely by your Year 1 ranking. Your LSAT score came next in importance, also nudging the odds toward a fail prediction since it was slightly under what most admitted students score. Family income ranked fourth and contributed similarly toward the unfavorable outcome, while your above-average undergrad GPA was fifth—still tilting things negatively in the model's assessment for your specific combination of traits. Your racial background placed sixth, part-time status seventh, and gender eighth, each adding a small push in the same direction. In essence, no feature strongly argued for success here; instead, your lower early academic rankings stood out most against typical students who pass the bar. Compared to peers, your school deciles are notably weaker, your LSAT edges below average, and part-time plans are uncommon among high performers. The good news is some of this is changeable: retaking the LSAT to boost your score could make a real difference, as it's a top influencer. Focusing intensely on law school coursework right away might help climb those deciles in future years, since early performance weighed so heavily. Switching to full-time study could also align better with bar-passing patterns. We encourage reapplying after addressing these—many students improve and succeed. Hang in there; this is just one data point, and your strong GPA shows real potential.
